content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 10, 2000

Mark P. Wald
Deputy City Attorney
City of Oakland
One Frank H. Ogawa Plaza, 6th Floor
Oakland, California 94612

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-302

Dear Mr. Wald:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Christopher Buckley regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

May Mr. Buckley participate in decisions concerning implementation of the Historic Preservation Element of the Oakland General Plan?

CONCLUSION

The facts presented to the Commission do not establish that "significant substantive review" will take place between Mr. Buckley's advice and any final governmental decision. Thus, while Mr. Buckley may conclude otherwise on fuller information, it appears at this point that he would be participating in the making of governmental decisions in which his real property interest is directly involved. Mr. Buckley will be disqualified from any such participation unless he can show that the decisions in question would have no foreseeable effect whatsoever on his real property interest. The Commission cannot determine the effects that these decisions would have on properties within the preservation zone, and leaves that determination to Mr. Buckley.

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18996, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-99-302
Page 2

FACTS

Christopher Buckley is a Planner III, for the City of Oakland's Community and
Economic Development Agency. Mr. Buckley has been asked by the city's planning director to
assist in implementing the Historic Preservation Element of the Oakland General Plan (the
"Element"). Mr. Buckley owns a one-family investment property at 2100 10th Avenue in the
S-7 preservation zone. His property and other S-7 properties could be affected by the Element's
implementation. Although Mr. Buckley's property is in the S-7 zone, the property is not a
designated city landmark. There are about 20 properties in the 10th Avenue S-7 zone, and about
102 properties in the S-7 zone citywide.

Mr. Buckley's work would consist of drafting recommendations for implementing
various action steps, for review and approval by his supervisors, including the deputy planning
director for strategic planning and the planning director, and presentation to the landmarks board
and city planning commission for their review and recommendation.

In most instances, the ultimate decisionmaking body for the Element's implementation
would be the city council. However, Mr. Buckley would not make any presentations to the city
council; the planning director or the deputy planning director performs that function.

The specific tasks that Mr. Buckley may perform are listed as action steps in the Element
as described below. All of these steps will need city council approval, with the exception of
Action 2.4.1 (landmark and preservation district design guidelines) which requires either
planning commission or city council approval, and Action 2.6.10 (apply State Historical
Building Code to designated historic properties and other qualified historical buildings) which
requires approval by a city building official or by the city manager.

Action 2.1.1: Amend zoning text to incorporate new preservation incentives and
regulations.

Action 2.1.2: Redesignate existing landmarks and preservation districts.²
Reclassify existing preservation study list properties as Heritage
Properties.

Action 2.4.1: Develop landmark and preservation district design guidelines.

Action 2.6.1: Adopt a Mills Act contract program.

² According to the facts provided in your letter, the term "preservation district" refers to the S-7 zone as
modified by Element implementation.

File No. A-99-302
Page 3

Action 2.6.2: Establish procedures for acceptance of conservation easements.

Action 2.6.3: Amend the Zoning Regulations to provide transferable
development rights.

Action 2.6.4: Amend the Zoning Regulations to limit existing zoning provisions
conditionally permitting additional uses in historic buildings.

Action 2.6.5: Investigate additional zoning amendments to broaden the range of
conditionally permitted uses for landmarks and preservation
districts in certain zones.

Action 2.6.7: Study the feasibility of establishing a historic preservation
revolving fund.

Action 2.6.8: Investigate establishing a Marks historical rehabilitation bond
program.

Action 2.6.10: Apply State Historical Building Code to designated historic
properties' and other qualified historical buildings.

ANALYSIS

Your questions concern the Act's conflict of interest provisions, which prohibit public
officials from making, participating in making, or in any way attempting to use their official
positions to influence governmental decisions in which they have financial interests. (Section
87100.) In particular, your questions focus on Mr. Buckley's place in the decisionmaking chain,
and the possible application of the "public generally exception" to the Act's conflict of interest
rules. The following discussion is organized to highlight these areas.

I. Mr. Buckley's participation in governmental decisionmaking.

The Act's conflict of interest rules apply only to public officials. As a Planner III for the
city's economic development agency, Mr. Buckley is certainly a public official governed by
these provisions. (Section 82048.) The prohibition of Section 87100 applies to specific conduct
by public officials—making, participating in making, or using one's official position to influence
a governmental decision. (Regulations 18702.1-18702.4.) For example, an official "makes a

3 According to the facts provided in your letter, the term "designated historic property" refers to properties
within preservation districts, as well as other historic properties (landmarks and heritage properties) given special
regulatory protection and preservation incentives under the Element.

File No. A-99-302
Page 4

governmental decision" when, among other things, he or she votes on a matter, obligates or
commits the city to any course of action, enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of the
city, or determines not to do any of those things. (Regulation 18702.1.)

More pertinent to Mr. Buckley, an official participates in making a governmental
decision when the official "advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker either
directly or without significant substantive review." (Regulation 18702.2 (b).) This advice or
recommendation may take the form of research or an investigation requiring the exercise of
judgment by the official, the purpose of which is to influence a governmental decision.
(Regulation 18702.2(b)(1).) Alternatively, it may take the form of a report, analysis, or opinion,
delivered orally or in writing, which requires the exercise of the official's judgment, and the
purpose of which is to influence a governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2(b)(2).)

You argue that many, at least, of the tasks assigned to Mr. Buckley would not amount to
participation in a governmental decision, due to the intervention of "significant substantive
review." To the extent that this is true, the conflicts analysis may come to an abrupt conclusion
if indeed Mr. Buckley is not participating in any governmental decisions. There seems to be no
question that decisions on implementation of the Element are "governmental decisions" within
the meaning of the Act. However, you observe in your letter that:

"In sum, the Deputy Planning Director, Planning Director,
Landmarks Board and City Planning Commission will review
Mr. Buckley's recommendations and exercise their independent
judgment. Moreover, except for design guidelines (Action 2.4.1)
and State Historic Building Code (Action 2.6.10) items, the City
Council must ultimately approve any recommendations and
Mr. Buckley would not present those."

Although you describe layers of review between Mr. Buckley and the ultimate
decisionmaker, you do not establish that any such review will in fact be either "significant" or
"substantive." The existence of some "review" or "significant, substantive review" may at times warrant a finding
that a particular official did not participate in a particular decision, but the Commission has
throughout its history interpreted this exception narrowly, emphasizing the substance of any
"review." The required analysis is stated succinctly in the Kaplan Advice Letter, No. A-82-108
(referring to officials whose status derived from their service as a consultant) as follows:

"It is our interpretation that a consultant participates in a decision,
even if it is 'reviewed' by several of his superiors, if those
superiors rely on the data or analysis prepared by the consultant
without checking it independently, if they rely on the professional
judgment of the consultant, or if the consultant in some other way
actually may influence the final decision. ...Making

File No. A-99-302
Page 5

recommendations to the final decision-makers, either directly or
indirectly, is exactly what participating in a governmental decision
means."

A public official, in short, participates in a governmental decision whenever he or she
transmits advice or recommendations to the ultimate decisionmaker, unless that advice or
recommendation is independently analyzed and reviewed from top to bottom. Any substantive
reliance on the work of the subordinate official makes that official a participant in the decision.
The only qualification to this rule applies when the subordinate official provides purely technical
assistance, which (a) is neither intended nor likely to influence the decision or (b) which is purely
ministerial in nature. An example of the first category of technical assistance would be advice on
the type of computer system to use in developing a long-range plan. An example of the second
category of technical assistance would be solution of a complex mathematical problem which,
although requiring expertise, has only one "right" answer. (Kaplan Advice Letter, supra.)4

If Mr. Buckley can satisfy himself that any of his "action steps" will in fact be subject to
the "significant substantive review" necessary to remove him from the decisionmaking chain, he
may offer his input without fear of a conflict of interest under the Act. We emphasize, however,
that he will have to determine for himself whether, in each "action step," he is providing purely
"technical assistance" or, if not, whether the ultimate decisionmaker will be relying to any extent
on his (Mr. Buckley's) professional judgment. Any recommendation which does not undergo
"significant substantive review" (as described above) amounts to participation by Mr. Buckley in
the governmental decision, potentially giving rise to a conflict of interest.

2. Is there any foreseeable effect on Mr. Buckley's property?

If Mr. Buckley participates in any governmental decisions, he must next decide whether
he has a financial interest in any of those decisions. An official has a financial interest in a
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on (among other enumerated economic
interests) any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $1,000 or
more in fair market value. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)

Mr. Buckley holds title (whether separately or as community property) to real property
located at 2100 10th Avenue, within the city's S-7 Zone. We will presume that the value of his

4 You also refer to Mr. Buckley's tasks as "implementation" of the Element. Decisions implementing
prior decisions do not give rise to conflicts of interest when the implementing decision cannot independently cause
reasonably foreseeable, material financial effects on any of the official's economic interests. The potential effects
of an implementing decision may not be ignored, however, simply because they implement a prior policy decision.
(Curry Advice Letter, No. I-98-006.) We cannot avoid analysis of the effects of such decisions.

File No. A-99-302
Page 6

interest in this real property is $1,000 or more. If so, he has an economic interest requiring
further analysis.5

After an official identifies an economic interest, he or she must determine whether it is
"reasonably foreseeable" that the decision in question will have a material financial effect on that
interest. First, the official decides whether the economic interest is directly or indirectly involved
in the decision. Commission regulations provide that real property is directly involved in a
governmental decision when, among other things, "[t]he decision involves the zoning or
rezoning, annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or exclusion from
any city, county, district or other local governmental subdivision, of real property in which the
official has a direct or indirect interest (other than a leasehold interest) of $1,000 or more, or a
similar decision affecting such property." (Regulation 18704.2(a)(1).)

The various tasks that Mr. Buckley may perform all seem ancillary to governmental
decisions that would amend the Element by selecting implementation options not originally
mandated. Because Mr. Buckley's property is within the preservation zone, that property is
directly involved in such decisions under the plain language of Regulation 18704.2(a)(1).
When an economic interest in real property is directly involved in a decision, any reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on the real property resulting from the decision is deemed material.
(Regulation 18705.2(a).)

As used here, "reasonably foreseeable" means "substantially likely." (Regulation 18706;
In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the financial consequences of a governmental
decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made is highly fact-specific, making
this evaluation a "judgment call." A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered
reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to meet the standard.
On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (Ibid.)

The question for Mr. Buckley is whether any of the governmental decisions in which he
may participate are substantially likely to have any financial effect on his real property interest.
If the answer is "yes," he has a conflict of interest in that decision, unless the "public generally"
exception applies. If the answer is "no," he does not have a conflict.

Historical preservation districts, like redevelopment projects, are intended to preserve or
increase the value of properties within their boundaries. It is therefore reasonable to infer that a
preservation element within a city plan will have at least some financial effect on properties
within the preservation area. (Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Com. (1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 983, 991.) However, as the Downey court noted, the FPPC has not "adopt[ed] a

5 Mr. Buckley's interest in this real property is the only economic interest you have disclosed to us. Our
analysis is therefore limited to effects on that real property interest.

File No. A-99-302
Page 7

rule that ownership of property in a redevelopment area ... is disqualification per se from voting
on a redevelopment plan. Each case is decided on its own circumstances." (Ibid.) The same is
true with preservation districts. Thus, if Mr. Buckley decides that it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have no financial effect whatsoever on his real property interest, then he
would not have a conflict of interest in that decision. (Regulation 18705(c)(2).) In making this
determination, he should evaluate the effects of any decision on the neighborhood surrounding
his real property, including such effects as changes in traffic flow, view, privacy, intensity of use,
noise levels, air emissions, or similar factors that might translate into effects on the fair market
value of his real property interest, or on its development or income producing potential.

If he decides that it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have at least some
financial effect on his economic interest, he will have a conflict unless the "public generally"
exception applies.

3. Application of the "public generally" exception.

If the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect of a decision on the public official's
economic interest is indistinguishable "from its effect on the public generally," then the public
official does not have a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Section 87103; Regulations
18700(b)(7), 18707(a).) This rule is referred to as the "public generally" exception.

The reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on a public official's economic
interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally if it is also reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will affect a "significant segment" of the public "in substantially the
same manner" as it affects the public official's economic interest. (Regulation 18707(b)(1),(2).)
Typically, application of the public generally exception requires two closely related judgments.
Using rules found in the FPPC's regulations, the official must determine whether there is a
"significant segment" of the public which is likely to be affected by the governmental decision in
"substantially the same manner" as is the economic interest which is potentially creating the
conflict. (There are also special purpose versions of the public generally exception which may
apply in specific factual situations. (See Regulations 18707.1-18707.6).)

In Mr. Buckley's case, a "significant segment" may be comprised of:

●     10 percent or more of the population of the city, assuming that the agency he works for,
Oakland's Community and Economic Development Agency, has citywide jurisdiction.
(Regulation 18707(b)(1)(A)(i).)

●     10 percent or more of all property owners, all home owners or all households within the
agency's jurisdiction. (Regulation 18707(b)(1)(A)(ii).)

●     At least 5,000 individuals who are residents of the city, again assuming that the agency
employing Mr. Buckley has city-wide jurisdiction. (Regulation 18707(b)(1)(C).)

File No. A-99-302
Page 8

In rare cases, "exceptional circumstances" may allow the recognition of a significant
segment, within the meaning of the public generally exception, even if the numerical thresholds
otherwise established are not met. (Regulation 18707(b)(1)(D).) We are not aware of any such
"exceptional circumstances" in this case, however.

"Substantially the same manner" is defined in Regulation 18707(b)(2):

"(2) Substantially the Same Manner: The governmental decision
will affect the official's economic interest in substantially the same
manner as it will affect the significant segment identified in
subdivision (b)(1) of this regulation."

You have indicated that there are only 20 properties within the S-7 zone that includes
Mr. Buckley's property, and 102 such properties citywide. In a city the size of Oakland, it seems
most unlikely that the properties affected by decisions on the Historic Preservation Element
could amount to a "significant segment" of the public. If that is the case, then Mr. Buckley may
not undertake the tasks you have described to us, except to the extent that there is significant
substantive review at or before the decisionmaking level, and except to the extent that any
decision would have no foreseeable effect on Mr. Buckley's property interest.

If you have any other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Commission Counsel, Legal Division

financial effect on his economic interest, he will have a conflict unless the “public generally”
exception applies.
3. Application ofthe “public generally” exception.
If the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect of a decision on the public official’s
economic interest is indistinguishable “from its effect on the public generally,” then the public
official does not have a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Section 87103; Regulations
18700(b)(7), 18707(a).) This rule is referred to as the “public generally” exception.
The reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on a public official’s economic
interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally if it is also reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will affect a “significant segment” ofthe public “in substantially the
same manner” as it affects the public official’s economic interest. (Regulation 1 8707(b)(l),(2).)
Typically, application of the public generally exception requires two closely related judgments.
Using rules found in the FPPC’s regulations, the official must determine whether there is a
“significant segment” of the public which is likely to be affected by the governmental decision in
“substantially the same manner” as is the economic interest which is potentially creating the
conflict.
(There are also special purpose versions of the public generally exception which may
apply in specific factual situations. (See Regulations 18707.1-18707.6).)
In Mr. Buckley’s case, a “significant segment” may be comprised of:
•
10 percent or more of the population of the city, assuming that the agency he works for,
Oakland’s Community and Economic Development Agency, has citywide jurisdiction.
(Regulation 1 8707(b)(1)(A)(i).)
•
10 percent or more of all property owners, all home owners or all households within the
agency’s jurisdiction. (Regulation 1 8707(b)(1)(A)(ii).)
•
At least 5,000 individuals who are residents of the city, again assuming that the agency
employing Mr. ‘Buckley has city-wide jurisdiction. (Regulation 1 8707(b)(1)(C).)
In rare cases, “exceptional circumstances” may allow the recognition of a significant
segment, within the meaning of the public generally exception, even ifthe numerical thresholds
otherwise established are not met. (Regulation 18707(b)(l)(D).)
We are not aware of any such
“exceptional circumstances” in this case, however.
“Substantially the same manner” is defined in Regulation 18707(b)(2):
“(2) Substantially the Same Manner: The governmental decision
will affect the official’s economic interest in substantially the same
manner as it will affect the significant segment identified in
subdivision (b)(l) of this regulation.”
You have indicated that there are only 20 properties within the S-7 zone that includes
Mr. Buckley’s property, and 102 such properties citywide. In a city the size of Oakland, it seems
most unlikely that the properties affected by decisions on the Historic Preservation Element
could amount to a “significant segment” of the public. Ifthat is the case, then Mr. Buckley may
not undertake the tasks you have described to us, except to the extent that there is significant
substantive review at or before the decisionmaking level, and except to the extent that any
decision would have no foreseeable effect on Mr. Buckley’s property interest.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Mr. Buckley participate in decisions concerning implementation of the Historic
/reservation Element of the Oakland General Plan?

CONCLUSION: The facts presented to the Commission do not establish that “significant substantive
review” will take place between Mr. Buckley’s advice and any final governmental decision.
Thus, while Mr. Buckley may conclude otherwise on fuller information, it appears at this point
that he would be participating in the making of governmental decisions in which his real property
interest is directly involved. Mr. Buckley will be disqualified from any such participation unless
he can show that the decisions in question would have noforeseeable effect whatsoever on his
real property interest. The Commission cannot determine the effects that these decisions would
have on properties within the preservation zone, and leaves that determination to Mr. Buckley.Page 2
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 February 10, 2000 Mark P. Wald Deputy City Attorney City of Oakland One Frank H. Ogawa Plaza, 6th Floor Oakland, California 94612 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-99-302 Dear Mr. Wald: This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Christopher Buckley regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ QUESTION May Mr. Buckley participate in decisions concerning implementation of the Historic Preservation Element of the Oakland General Plan? CONCLUSION The facts presented to the Commission do not establish that "significant substantive review" will take place between Mr. Buckley's advice and any final governmental decision. Thus, while Mr. Buckley may conclude otherwise on fuller information, it appears at this point that he would be participating in the making of governmental decisions in which his real property interest is directly involved. Mr. Buckley will be disqualified from any such participation unless he can show that the decisions in question would have no foreseeable effect whatsoever on his real property interest. The Commission cannot determine the effects that these decisions would have on properties within the preservation zone, and leaves that determination to Mr. Buckley. ¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18996, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-99-302 Page 2 FACTS Christopher Buckley is a Planner III, for the City of Oakland's Community and Economic Development Agency. Mr. Buckley has been asked by the city's planning director to assist in implementing the Historic Preservation Element of the Oakland General Plan (the "Element"). Mr. Buckley owns a one-family investment property at 2100 10th Avenue in the S-7 preservation zone. His property and other S-7 properties could be affected by the Element's implementation. Although Mr. Buckley's property is in the S-7 zone, the property is not a designated city landmark. There are about 20 properties in the 10th Avenue S-7 zone, and about 102 properties in the S-7 zone citywide. Mr. Buckley's work would consist of drafting recommendations for implementing various action steps, for review and approval by his supervisors, including the deputy planning director for strategic planning and the planning director, and presentation to the landmarks board and city planning commission for their review and recommendation. In most instances, the ultimate decisionmaking body for the Element's implementation would be the city council. However, Mr. Buckley would not make any presentations to the city council; the planning director or the deputy planning director performs that function. The specific tasks that Mr. Buckley may perform are listed as action steps in the Element as described below. All of these steps will need city council approval, with the exception of Action 2.4.1 (landmark and preservation district design guidelines) which requires either planning commission or city council approval, and Action 2.6.10 (apply State Historical Building Code to designated historic properties and other qualified historical
